name: Weather & Climate
sourceAttribution: Compiled from NOAA National Weather Service, CDC public-health guidance on heat and cold, and public-domain meteorology references. NOT a substitute for official forecasts or local emergency guidance.
license: CC-BY-4.0 (compilation); source citations carry their own public-domain license

guides.title: Reading weather radar
guides.sourceRef: NOAA NWS — Understanding Doppler Radar imagery, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Color scale (typical):
• Green: light to moderate rain
• Yellow: heavy rain
• Orange: very heavy rain
• Red: torrential rain, possible hail
• Purple/pink: very intense, often hail

Key shapes to recognize:
• Hook echo: the radar return curls back on itself in a hook shape. Often associated with rotation and a possible tornado.
• Bow echo: a line of storms bent forward like an archer's bow. Usually marks strong straight-line wind (derecho).
• Squall line: a long, narrow line of intense storms moving as a unit.
• Bright bands or rings: can indicate hail or freezing levels.

A tight gradient (colors change rapidly across a small area) usually means stronger storms.

If you see a hook or bow echo near you on radar, do NOT keep watching the radar — take shelter and check after.

guides.title: Lightning safety — the 30/30 rule
guides.content:
If you hear thunder within 30 seconds of seeing lightning, the storm is within roughly 6 miles — close enough to be struck. Get inside immediately.

Wait 30 minutes after the last thunder before going back outside.

Indoors during a storm:
• Stay off corded phones (cordless and cell are fine)
• Don't use plumbing — no shower, no dish washing
• Stay away from windows
• Unplug sensitive electronics

Outdoors with no shelter (last resort):
• Avoid tall isolated objects (trees in the open, poles, towers)
• Stay off water and out of metal-frame structures
• Get off ridges and exposed peaks
• Crouch low with feet together — minimize ground contact and target height. Don't lie flat (more contact with ground current).

"When thunder roars, go indoors." If you can hear it, lightning can reach you. Most lightning deaths happen to people who waited too long to seek shelter.

guides.title: Heat illness — exhaustion vs stroke
guides.sourceRef: CDC Heat & Health Tracker — Heat-Related Illness, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Heat exhaustion:
• Heavy sweating, cool moist skin
• Weakness, fatigue
• Headache, nausea, dizziness
• Muscle cramps
• Body temperature normal or mildly elevated

Action: move to a cool place, loosen clothing, sip cool water, apply cool wet cloths or take a cool shower. Recovery is usually fast.

Heat stroke (life-threatening, call your emergency number):
• Body temperature 103°F (39.4°C) or higher
• Hot, red, dry OR damp skin (sweating may have stopped)
• Rapid strong pulse
• Confusion, slurred speech, agitation
• Possible loss of consciousness or seizure

Action: call emergency services immediately. Cool the person aggressively:
• Move to shade or air conditioning
• Cold packs (or ice in bags or cold-water-soaked cloth) on the neck, armpits, and groin where large blood vessels run
• Submerge in a cool bath if possible
• Do NOT give fluids if confused or unconscious — aspiration risk

Minutes matter. Heat stroke can cause brain damage or death within an hour.

guides.title: Cold illness — hypothermia and frostbite
guides.sourceRef: CDC Hypothermia and Frostbite + NOAA NWS Wind Chill safety, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Hypothermia stages:
• Mild: uncontrollable shivering, slurred speech, clumsy hands. Body temp 90–95°F (32–35°C).
• Moderate: shivering may stop, confusion, drowsiness, slow pulse.
• Severe: unconsciousness, weak or absent pulse. Body temp below 82°F (28°C).

Treatment (any stage — call your emergency number for moderate/severe):
• Get out of the cold and out of wind.
• Remove wet clothing; replace with dry layers.
• Warm the core first (chest, neck, head, groin) — not the extremities. Skin-to-skin contact in a sleeping bag works.
• Warm sweet drinks if conscious. No alcohol.
• Handle severely hypothermic person GENTLY — rough handling can trigger cardiac arrest.

Frostbite:
• Affected skin: numb, white or grayish, hard or waxy. Often fingers, toes, nose, ears.
• Get indoors.
• Warm the area with body heat (e.g., armpit) or warm — not hot — water (about body temperature).
• Do NOT rub. Do NOT use direct heat (fire, heating pad).
• Don't re-thaw if there's any chance of re-freezing — that causes more damage.
• Get medical care.

Wind chill makes cold dangerous fast even when air temperature looks manageable.

guides.title: Watch vs warning
guides.content:
These terms have specific meanings — they're not casual.

Watch: conditions are FAVORABLE for the hazard to develop within hours.
• Be alert.
• Review your plan.
• Monitor updates.
• Bring in outdoor items, charge devices, fuel up.

Warning: the hazard is OCCURRING, IMMINENT, or HIGHLY LIKELY in the warned area.
• Take protective action immediately.
• Shelter, evacuate, or whatever the specific warning calls for.

Advisory: a less severe condition that may still cause significant inconvenience or hazard. Use caution; specific guidance applies.

Delivery channels:
• Weather radio (NOAA Weather Radio — 7 frequencies in the US)
• Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) on your phone
• Local TV/radio
• Outdoor sirens (in tornado country)

If your phone goes off with a Wireless Emergency Alert, read it before dismissing. They're issued only for serious threats.

guides.title: Cloud-reading basics
guides.sourceRef: NOAA NWS Cloud Classification + public-domain meteorology references, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Reading the sky gives you weather information no radar can — what's happening right now, where you are.

Clouds to know:
• Cumulus: puffy white cotton-ball clouds in fair weather. Friendly.
• Cumulonimbus: tall, anvil-topped storm clouds. Means severe weather — rain, lightning, hail, sometimes tornadoes. Get inside.
• Mammatus: pouch-like bulges under a cloud (often after a thunderstorm). Indicates a recent severe storm, sometimes still developing.
• Stratus: low gray layer. Light drizzle, fog. Mild.
• Cirrus: high wispy 'horse-tail' clouds. Often precede a weather change in 24 hours.
• Lenticular: lens-shaped, often near mountains. High-altitude winds; turbulence aloft.

General patterns:
• Sky getting darker / clouds lowering = weather worsening soon.
• Wind shifting noticeably = front passing.
• Sudden temperature drop with cumulonimbus building = severe storm probable; seek shelter.
